section.title: Nathan Rebukes David
section.sectionRationale: The LORD refuses to leave David's sin hidden. He sends Nathan, who traps David with a parable about a rich man who stole a poor man's only lamb; when David condemns the man, Nathan declares, 'You are that man.' David confesses, is forgiven, and yet is told the consequences will fall on his own house, beginning with the death of the child. The movement holds together God's justice and God's mercy, real forgiveness alongside real consequences, and it turns on David's genuine repentance.
section.thesis: God will not let His king's sin lie buried; He sends His word through Nathan to expose it, and when David confesses, 'I have sinned against the LORD,' God takes away his sin, yet the consequences of his despising God's word still unfold in his house, so that grace and discipline, forgiveness and lasting fallout, are held together in truth.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Nathan's parable and the verdict 'you are that man,' verses 1 to 12; David's confession and God's forgiveness with announced consequences, verses 13 to 14; the death of the child and David's response, verses 15 to 23; and the birth of Solomon and the capture of Rabbah, verses 24 to 31.

verses.text: Then the LORD sent Nathan to David, and when he arrived, he said, “There were two men in a certain city, one rich and the other poor.
The rich man had a great number of sheep and cattle,

but the poor man had nothing except one small ewe lamb that he had bought. He raised it, and it grew up with him and his children. It shared his food and drank from his cup; it slept in his arms and was like a daughter to him.

Now a traveler came to the rich man, who refrained from taking one of his own sheep or cattle to prepare for the traveler who had come to him. Instead, he took the poor man’s lamb and prepared it for his guest.”
David burned with anger against the man and said to Nathan: “As surely as the LORD lives, the man who did this deserves to die!
Because he has done this thing and has shown no pity, he must pay for the lamb four times over.”
Then Nathan said to David, “You are that man! This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘I anointed you king over Israel, and I delivered you from the hand of Saul.
I gave your master’s house to you and your master’s wives into your arms. I gave you the house of Israel and Judah, and if that was not enough, I would have given you even more.

Why then have you {{bazah|despised}} the command of the LORD by doing evil in His sight? You put Uriah the Hittite to the sword and took his wife as your own. You have slain him with the sword of the Ammonites.
Now, therefore, the sword will never depart from your house, because you have despised Me and have taken the wife of Uriah the Hittite to be your own.’

This is what the LORD says: ‘I will raise up adversity against you from your own house. Before your very eyes I will take your wives and give them to another, and he will lie with them in broad daylight.
You have acted in secret, but I will do this thing in broad daylight before all Israel.’”

units.label: Confession and forgiveness
units.note: I have sinned against the LORD

verses.text: Then David said to Nathan, “I have {{chata|sinned}} against the LORD.” “The LORD has taken away your sin,” Nathan replied. “You will not die.
Nevertheless, because by this deed you have shown utter contempt for the word of the LORD, the son born to you will surely die.”
After Nathan had gone home, the LORD struck the child that Uriah’s wife had borne to David, and he became ill.
David pleaded with God for the boy. He fasted and went into his house and spent the night lying in sackcloth on the ground.
The elders of his household stood beside him to help him up from the ground, but he was unwilling and would not eat anything with them.

On the seventh day the child died. But David’s servants were afraid to tell him that the child was dead, for they said, “Look, while the child was alive, we spoke to him, and he would not listen to us. So how can we tell him the child is dead? He may even harm himself.”
When David saw that his servants were whispering to one another, he perceived that the child was dead. So he asked his servants, “Is the child dead?” “He is dead,” they replied.

Then David got up from the ground, washed and anointed himself, changed his clothes, and went into the house of the LORD and worshiped. Then he went to his own house, and at his request they set food before him, and he ate.
“What is this you have done?” his servants asked. “While the child was alive, you fasted and wept, but when he died, you got up and ate.”
David answered, “While the child was alive, I fasted and wept, for I said, ‘Who knows? The LORD may be gracious to me and let him live.’
But now that he is dead, why should I fast? Can I bring him back again? I will go to him, but he will not return to me.”
Then David comforted his wife Bathsheba, and he went to her and lay with her. So she gave birth to a son, and they named him Solomon. Now the LORD loved the child
and sent word through Nathan the prophet to name him Jedidiah because the LORD loved him.
Meanwhile, Joab fought against Rabbah of the Ammonites and captured the royal fortress.
Then Joab sent messengers to David to say, “I have fought against Rabbah and have captured the water supply of the city.
Now, therefore, assemble the rest of the troops, lay siege to the city, and capture it. Otherwise I will capture the city, and it will be named after me.”
So David assembled all the troops and went to Rabbah; and he fought against it and captured it.

Then he took the crown from the head of their king. It weighed a talent of gold and was set with precious stones, and it was placed on David’s head. And David took a great amount of plunder from the city.

David brought out the people who were there and put them to work with saws, iron picks, and axes, and he made them work at the brick kilns. He did the same to all the Ammonite cities. Then David and all his troops returned to Jerusalem.

bazah.gloss: To despise, hold in contempt, treat as worthless. Nathan says David has despised the command of the LORD, and even despised the LORD Himself, verses 9 and 10. The word cuts to the root of David's sin: not merely weakness but a contempt for God's word, treating the Giver of everything as though His word could be brushed aside.

chata.gloss: To sin, to miss the mark, to offend against God. David's confession is stripped to three Hebrew words, chatati la-YHWH, 'I have sinned against the LORD,' with no excuse, no blame shifted. It is the turning point of the chapter, the honest naming of sin as sin before God, which David expands in Psalm 51 and which meets God's ready forgiveness.

basic.context: Chapter 11 ended with David's sin hidden and God's verdict that it was evil. Chapter 12 opens with the LORD refusing to leave it buried: He sends the prophet Nathan. Nathan tells David a story about a rich man with vast flocks who, rather than use his own, stole and slaughtered a poor man's beloved only lamb to feed a guest. David, not seeing himself in it, erupts in anger and declares the man deserves to die and must repay fourfold. Nathan springs the trap: 'You are that man.' He recounts all God had given David and charges him with despising the LORD's word by murdering Uriah and taking his wife. He announces lasting consequences: the sword will never leave David's house, and his own household will rise against him publicly. David does not argue or deflect; he says simply, 'I have sinned against the LORD.' Nathan announces that God has taken away his sin so he will not die, yet the child born of the sin will die. David pleads and fasts for the child for seven days, but the child dies. David then rises, worships, and eats, explaining that he cannot bring the child back but will one day go to him. God gives David and Bathsheba another son, Solomon, whom the LORD loves and names Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD. The chapter closes with the capture of Rabbah. It raises the questions of how God brings a sinner to repentance, how forgiveness and consequences fit together, and what true repentance looks like.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that teaches profound truths about sin, confession, forgiveness, and consequences. Nathan's parable models how God's word can slip past our defenses to convict us. The chapter carefully holds together two things people often pull apart: God fully forgives David's sin, yet real consequences still unfold. The death of the child is a hard providence tied to David's sin, not a general rule that God takes children for parents' sins; David's confidence that he will go to the child, verse 23, has long comforted grieving parents with hope beyond death. The chapter is description of God's dealings with David, from which we learn about God's character.
basic.originalLanguages: Nathan's charge names the root of David's sin with the word bazah, to despise: David has despised the command of the LORD and despised the LORD Himself, treating the word of the God who gave him everything as worthless. David's answer is stripped bare to the Hebrew chatati, 'I have sinned,' no excuse, no blame shifted, the honest naming of sin as sin before the LORD. Between contempt and confession lies the whole drama of repentance, and God meets the confession at once with forgiveness.
basic.typology: Nathan the prophet, sent by God to expose sin and call the king to repentance, pictures the prophetic word that convicts and leads to life. David's confession and forgiveness, sin taken away yet consequences remaining, points to the deeper resolution in Christ, who bears not only the guilt but the full penalty of sin. And the child of David loved by the LORD, named beloved, in the line that leads to Solomon and ultimately to Christ, shows grace triumphing over judgment.
basic.christ: The gospel truth of this chapter is that God takes away David's sin, but Someone must still bear it. David is forgiven and does not die, yet the wages of sin are real, seen in the death that follows. This points forward to Christ, on whom the full penalty of sin like David's was laid, so that God could be both just and the justifier of the guilty. Nathan's 'the LORD has taken away your sin' finds its ground at the cross, where the sin was not ignored but carried by the sinless Son of David.
basic.god: God is revealed as both just and merciful. He is just: He will not leave sin hidden, He confronts His king through His word, and He does not pretend the sin never happened, allowing real consequences. He is merciful: He seeks out the sinner rather than destroying him, forgives immediately upon confession, and even names the next son 'beloved of the LORD,' weaving grace into the aftermath of sin. God is the one who sends the word that wounds in order to heal.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 51 is David's fuller prayer of repentance from this moment. Psalm 32 celebrates the blessedness of forgiven sin. Galatians 6 warns that we reap what we sow, as David does here. And 1 John 1 promises that if we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive, the pattern seen in David's confession.
basic.oneStory: God will not leave His king in his sin; He sends His word to convict, receives David's confession, and takes away his sin, even as the consequences ripple through his house. This holding together of full forgiveness and real consequences finds its resolution only in Christ, the beloved Son of David, on whom the penalty of forgiven sin was laid, so that God is both just and the one who justifies the repentant.

context.literary: The chapter answers chapter 11 point for point: the hidden becomes exposed, the silent verdict 'evil' becomes Nathan's spoken indictment, and David's taking is met with God's taking within his house. Nathan's parable is a masterpiece of prophetic strategy, drawing David's own judgment on himself before the reversal 'you are that man.' The scene of the child's death and David's surprising response, and the birth of the beloved Solomon, round off the movement with grief and grace intertwined.
context.historical: Prophets confronting kings was a recognized, and dangerous, role in Israel, and Nathan's use of a legal-sounding case invited a royal judgment. Fourfold restitution reflects the law's penalty for stolen sheep, Exodus 22:1, a judgment David unwittingly passes on himself, and which some see darkly fulfilled as four of his children die. Mourning customs, fasting, sackcloth, and lying on the ground, frame David's plea. The capture of Rabbah and the king's crown mark the end of the Ammonite war that framed the whole crisis.
context.canonical: This chapter, with Psalm 51, becomes the Bible's classic account of repentance and forgiveness, shaping the doctrine of confession throughout Scripture. The consequences announced here, the sword in David's house and public shame, unfold across chapters 13 to 20. Solomon, named Jedidiah, carries the covenant line forward toward the temple and toward Christ, showing that God's grace and covenant faithfulness are not undone by David's sin.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how God, in justice and mercy, exposes David's hidden sin through His prophetic word, brings him to genuine confession, freely forgives him, and yet allows real consequences to unfold, so that readers grasp both the seriousness of sin and the readiness of God's grace toward the repentant.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's particular dealings with David, including the hard providence of the child's death tied to this specific sin; it does not prescribe that God always takes children for a parent's sin. What endures is the pattern of conviction, honest confession, and free forgiveness, the truth that forgiven sin may still carry consequences, and the hope David voices of going to be with his child beyond death.

debates.question: Why does the child die if David is forgiven?

views.label: Forgiveness removes guilt but not all temporal consequences
views.case: Nathan says plainly 'the LORD has taken away your sin; you will not die,' so David's guilt is fully pardoned, yet the announced consequence, the child's death, still falls; Scripture teaches that God forgives freely while a sinner may still reap what he has sown, Galatians 6:7, so the child's death displays the seriousness of sin and its ripple of consequence, even as David himself is spared and restored.

views.label: A hard providence displaying sin's gravity, not a rule about children
views.case: Others stress that this is a specific, sovereign act tied to David's unique sin and role, not a general principle that God takes children for parents' faults; the point is the costliness of David's sin, publicly answering the private evil, while David's words in verse 23, 'I will go to him,' hold out hope that the child is safe with God, so that judgment and mercy meet even here.

debates.bearing: Both agree the child's death is not a denial of David's forgiveness but a real consequence of grievous sin, and both find comfort in David's confidence of reunion beyond death; the chapter warns that sin is costly even when forgiven, while assuring that God's mercy holds, ultimately grounding forgiveness at the cross where the full penalty was borne by Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Nathan's indictment fastens on one devastating word: bazah, to despise. David has despised the command of the LORD, verse 9, and, more pointedly still, despised the LORD Himself, verse 10, treating the word of the God who had given him everything as worthless. That is the root beneath the adultery and the murder, contempt for God's word. David's response is reduced to the barest, most honest Hebrew, chatati la-YHWH, 'I have sinned against the LORD,' verse 13, two words that carry no excuse and shift no blame, the true confession that Psalm 51 will unfold at length. And the moment the word of contempt gives way to the word of confession, God's forgiveness meets it: 'the LORD has taken away your sin.' The chapter's whole theology of repentance turns on the movement from bazah to chata honestly named, from despising God's word to owning one's sin before Him, and on the astonishing readiness of God's grace to forgive.

typology.type: The prophet sent to expose sin and call to repentance
typology.fulfillment: The word of God that convicts and leads to life, wielded fully by Christ and His Spirit, John 16:8 and Hebrews 4:12.

typology.type: Sin forgiven yet its penalty still borne
typology.fulfillment: Christ, on whom the full penalty of forgiven sin was laid, so God is both just and justifier, Romans 3:26 and Isaiah 53:5.

typology.type: The beloved son in the line of promise
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the truly Beloved Son, in whom the Father is well pleased, the goal of the line running through Solomon, Matthew 3:17.

study.christ: The deepest gospel note of this chapter is that God takes away David's sin, yet the sin is not simply ignored, its penalty is real, seen in the death that follows. This points forward to Christ, on whom the full penalty of sin like David's was laid, so that God could be both just and the justifier of the guilty who trust Him, Romans 3:26. Nathan's word, 'the LORD has taken away your sin,' finds its righteous ground at the cross, where the sin of God's people was not overlooked but carried by the sinless Son of David, Isaiah 53:5. And the grace that names the next child Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD, and carries the covenant line on to Christ, shows that God's mercy triumphs over judgment, weaving even the aftermath of grievous sin into His saving purpose.
study.god: God is revealed as perfectly just and richly merciful, and this chapter refuses to let us choose between them. He is just: He will not leave sin hidden, He sends His word to confront His own beloved king, and He does not pretend the evil never happened but lets real consequences fall. He is merciful: He seeks out the sinner rather than destroying him, forgives the moment David confesses, spares his life, and even names the next son 'beloved of the LORD,' weaving grace into the very aftermath of sin. God is the God who sends the word that wounds in order to heal, who disciplines those He loves, and whose forgiveness is as ready as His holiness is uncompromising.

crossReferences.note: David's fuller prayer of repentance arising from this confrontation, pleading for mercy, cleansing, and a clean heart.
The blessedness of the one whose sin is forgiven, and the relief of confession over concealment, drawn from this experience.
A person reaps what he sows, the principle behind the real consequences that follow David's forgiven sin.
If we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive us, the very pattern of David's confession and God's response.

study.oneStory: God will not leave His king buried in his sin; He sends His prophetic word to convict, receives David's honest confession, and takes away his sin, even as the announced consequences begin to ripple through his house. This holding together of full forgiveness and real consequence finds its final resolution only in Christ, the beloved Son of David, on whom the penalty of forgiven sin was laid, so that God is shown to be both just and the justifier of all who, like David, turn from despising His word to confessing their sin before Him. Even here, grace has the last word, in the beloved son who carries the promise on toward the Messiah.

quiz.q: How does Nathan bring David to see his own sin?

options.text: He tells a parable about a rich man stealing a poor man's only lamb, and after David condemns the man, declares, 'You are that man.'
options.note: Verses 1-7 show Nathan's parable drawing David's own judgment onto himself.

options.text: He openly accuses David of adultery and murder from the start.
options.note: Nathan does confront David directly, but only after the parable has led David to condemn himself.

options.text: He uses a story that leads David to pronounce judgment on himself unawares.
options.note: True, but this option omits the dramatic turn 'you are that man' that makes the confrontation land.

options.text: He waits silently until David confesses on his own.
options.note: Nathan actively confronts David with a parable and a direct charge; David does not confess unprompted.

quiz.q: What is the root of David's sin according to Nathan?

options.text: He despised the command of the LORD, and despised the LORD Himself, treating God's word as worthless.
options.note: Verses 9-10 name David's contempt for God's word as the root of his sin.

options.text: He simply gave in to a moment of weakness he could not help.
options.note: Nathan calls it despising the LORD, not an unavoidable weakness.

options.text: He disregarded God's word despite all God had given him.
options.note: True, but this option omits the sharp charge that David despised the LORD Himself, not only His command.

options.text: He failed to consult the LORD before acting.
options.note: The issue is contempt for God's known command, not merely a failure to inquire.

quiz.q: How does God respond to David's confession, 'I have sinned against the LORD'?

options.text: He takes away David's sin so he will not die, yet announces that real consequences will still fall on his house.
options.note: Verses 13-14 hold together full forgiveness and real consequences.

options.text: He withholds forgiveness until David proves his repentance over time.
options.note: Forgiveness is immediate upon confession; God does not make David earn it.

options.text: He forgives David freely but lets consequences remain.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific assurance 'you will not die' and the announced consequences to his house.

options.text: He forgives David and removes every consequence of the sin.
options.note: God forgives but does not remove all consequences; the child dies and the sword remains in David's house.

quiz.q: How does David respond after the child dies?

options.text: He rises, worships, and eats, saying he cannot bring the child back but will one day go to him.
options.note: Verses 20-23 show David's worship and his hope of reunion beyond death.

options.text: He refuses to accept the child's death and continues fasting in despair.
options.note: The opposite; David stops fasting once the child dies and worships God.

options.text: He gets up, worships God, and returns to eating.
options.note: True, but this option omits David's striking words of hope, 'I will go to him, but he will not return to me.'

options.text: He blames God for taking the child and turns away in anger.
options.note: David worships rather than blaming God, submitting to God's will with hope.

quiz.q: How does the chapter hold together God's justice and mercy in a way that points to Christ?

options.text: God forgives David yet the penalty is real, foreshadowing Christ who bore the full penalty of forgiven sin so God is just and justifier.
options.note: The pattern of sin taken away yet penalty borne points to the cross, Romans 3:26.

options.text: It shows that God forgives sin without any cost to anyone.
options.note: The chapter shows sin has real cost; ultimately the cost is borne by Christ, not ignored.

options.text: It reveals a God who is both just in judging sin and merciful in forgiving the repentant.
options.note: True, but this option stops short of the gospel resolution that Christ bears the penalty of forgiven sin.

options.text: It teaches that repentance alone fully satisfies God's justice apart from any atonement.
options.note: David's forgiveness is grounded, in the whole Bible's testimony, in the atonement Christ provides, not in repentance apart from it.

quiz.q: What does the birth and naming of Solomon reveal after this dark episode?

options.text: That God's grace and covenant faithfulness continue, as the LORD loves the child and names him Jedidiah, beloved of the LORD.
options.note: Verses 24-25 show grace triumphing, the covenant line carried on in the beloved Solomon.

options.text: That David's sin had permanently forfeited God's covenant promises.
options.note: The opposite; God's covenant continues, and the beloved Solomon carries the line toward Christ.

options.text: That God still blesses David's house despite his sin.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking detail that the LORD loved the child and gave him the name Jedidiah.

options.text: That Solomon's birth simply replaced the child who had died.
options.note: Solomon is more than a replacement; he is the beloved of the LORD who carries the covenant promise forward.

questions: Nathan's parable slipped past David's defenses so he judged himself. Are there sins you readily condemn in others while excusing the same thing in yourself?
David's confession was stripped bare: 'I have sinned against the LORD,' with no excuse. What would it look like for you to confess a sin that plainly, without blame shifting?
God forgave David fully, yet consequences still unfolded. How do you hold on to the reality of God's complete forgiveness even while living with the fallout of past sin?
David rose, worshiped, and rested in the hope that he would go to his child. How does the hope of God's mercy beyond death steady you in grief and loss?
God wove grace into the aftermath of David's sin, naming Solomon 'beloved of the LORD.' Where have you seen God bring unexpected grace out of a season marked by your failure?

translationNotes.note: The BSB follows the Dead Sea Scrolls reading 'you have shown utter contempt for the word of the LORD,' while the Masoretic Text reads 'you have brought utter contempt from the enemies of the LORD'; either way the sin gave occasion for contempt toward God.

David's words 'I will go to him, but he will not return to me' express hope of reunion beyond death, long a comfort to grieving parents, resting in the mercy of God rather than presuming on David's own merit.
The BSB footnote explains that Jedidiah means 'beloved of the LORD'; the name given to Solomon signals God's love and covenant favor resting on the child despite the sin that preceded his birth.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 12:1-31. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced in this file. David's sin, forgiveness, and consequences are handled as description of God's dealings, not prescription.
